rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE NG et al VS. CALIFORNIA PARKING COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Plaintiffs Motion To Disqualify Defendants Expert Carol Beebe Walser, Ph.D.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, February 27, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFFS KATHERINE NG, RICKY NG'S Motion To Disqualify Defendants Expert Carol Beebe Walser, Ph.D. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This motion is continued to March 4, 2015 at 10:00 a.m. to give Defendants the opportunity to comply with Local Rule 2.6B. Defendants must deliver courtesy copies of the opposition papers to Douglas Robbins, 1 Post Street, Suite 800, San Francisco, California 94104, with a cover letter stating the new hearing date, no later than February 27, 2015 at 4:00 pm or the motion will be granted. Any exhibit must be separated by an exhibit tab per California Rules of Court 3.1110(f). The court has courtesy copies of the moving papers and reply brief. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GARY DWAYNE FAW et al VS. UNITED PARCEL SERVICE OF AMERICA, INC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 27 2015, Line 8, DEFENDANT UNITED PARCEL SERVICE, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion. Motion denied. First, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether UPS breached any duty to Plaintiffs. UPS concedes that, as the owner of the lift, it had a duty to ensure that the lift was in a safe and operable working condition. Plaintiff's testimony does not establish that UPS did not breach that duty. Additionally, Plaintiffs have produced unrebutted evidence that the lift was defective and inappropriate for the use authorized by UPS. (Smith Decl. at 4:4-7, 4:16-18.) Second, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether UPS's alleged negligence caused Plaintiffs' injuries. UPS argues only that there is insufficient direct evidence of causation, and that direct evidence cannot be obtained. However, Plaintiffs have produced unrebutted circumstantial evidence that their injuries were caused either by UPS's negligent authorization of use of the lift for the specific job, or by UPS's negligence in maintaining the lift in a safe and operable working condition. (Smith Decl. at 3-5.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: MATTHEW WANG DBA CTW MECHANICAL VS. GREEN APPLE, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Disqualify Harvey W. Stein And Law Offices Of Harvey W. Stein
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 27 2015, Line 9, DEFENDANT GREEN APPLE, INC., Motion To Disqualify Harvey W. Stein And Law Offices Of Harvey W. Stein. Motion denied. It is clear from the record that the Motion could and should have been made concurrently with or shortly after the hearing on the motion to set aside default. No such action was taken, nor did Defendant avail himself of the opportunity to do so until thirteen months after Ung was first served, six months after counsel was retained, six months after Ung's declaration was filed in support of his motion to set aside default, five days before Ung's deposition, and within a few weeks of trial. Stein's connection with the adversary party was revealed in Ung's Declaration filed six months ago in support of his motion to set aside default. Alternatively, the Court finds that no substantial relationship exists to warrant disqualification. Stein did not represent Ung in an individual capacity at his deposition, but only in a representative capacity as an officer of Icebee, and Ung revealed no confidential information to Stein during the Icebee litigation. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: PATRICK LONSDALE ET AL VS. KSL CAPITAL PARTNERS LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO TRANSFER
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 27 2015, Line 13, DEFENDANT KSL CAPITAL PARTNERS LLC, SQUAW VALLEY RESORT LLC, SQUAW VALLEY SKI HOLDINGS LLC, JMA VENTURES LLC MOTION TO TRANSFER. Denied. Case law establishes two factors that the court must consider in determining whether a defendant is joined solely for venue purposes - (1) whether the plaintiff added the defendant in good faith and (2) whether it would be possible for a cause of action to be pled against the defendant. (Gutierrez v. Sup. Ct. (1966) 243 Cal.App.2d 710, 725.) The Court finds that Plaintiffs' evidence establishes that Defendant JMA Ventures, LLC ("JMA") was added as a defendant in good faith. The Declaration of Andrew McDevitt shows that Plaintiffs had a reasonable basis for alleging that JMA is involved in the ownership or operation of Squaw Valley Resort. (McDevitt Decl., Exs. 3, 5.) If the plaintiff has "reasonable grounds for the belief in good faith that plaintiff had a cause of action against the resident defendant¿then we do not believe that it may be said that the resident defendant was improperly joined as a defendant or was made a defendant solely for the purpose of having the action tried in the county of the residence of the resident defendant within the meaning of section 395 of the Code of Civil Procedure." (Peiser v. Mettler (1958) 50 Cal.2d 594, 603.) Some cases also require a showing that the plaintiff can conceivably state a cause of action against the defendant in order to survive a motion to transfer venue. However, it is a low standard and the "trial court does not need to try, upon conflicting affidavits, the issues of facts going to the merits of the cause of action stated against the resident defendant." (California Collection Agency v. Fontana (1943) 61 Cal.App.2d 648, 653.) "Allegations which merely shadow forth the semblance of a cause of action against such defendant are proof against assault on the ground that they are sham and frivolous, provided such cause of action is apparently pleaded in good faith and is not prima facie so glaringly and vitally defective as to be beyond correction by amendment." (Gutierrez v. Sup. Ct. (1966) 243 Cal.App.2d 710, 726, internal quotations omitted.) The allegations against JMA are not so "glaringly and vitally defective" that the Court can declare JMA a sham defendant.
